PROJ-210 -- Migrate auth from JWT to session cookies
Status: In Progress  |  Priority: High  |  Due: 2026-05-28
Assignee: @me (Zoe Dev)  |  Estimate: 13 pts  |  Team: Backend + Frontend
URL: https://linear.app/zuno/issue/PROJ-210
Created: 2026-05-15 by Sarah Chen
Labels: auth, security, full-stack, breaking-change, sprint-2026-05

Description:
Security requirement: replace JWT tokens (stored in browser localStorage)
with secure session cookies (httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict). The
current auth flow stores the JWT in localStorage, making it vulnerable
to XSS attacks. If any script on the page can read localStorage, it can
steal the JWT and impersonate the user.

This issue was flagged in the 2026-Q1 security review by our external auditor
(ref: audit-2026-q1, finding #7). Must be resolved by 2026-05-28.

Acceptance Criteria:
  [ ] New logins use httpOnly session cookies
  [ ] httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict flags are set on all cookies
  [ ] CSRF token is generated on login and validated on mutations
  [ ] Feature flag AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED controls the rollout
  [ ] Existing JWT sessions continue to work during transition
  [ ] SSO (Okta) flow is updated to use cookies
  [ ] All integration tests pass with cookies
  [ ] Deployed to staging and verified

Technical Scope:

  FRONTEND (Alex Rodriguez leads):
    File: src/context/AuthContext.tsx
      - Remove: const jwt = localStorage.getItem('auth_token')
      - Remove: axios.defaults.headers.Authorization = `Bearer ${jwt}`
      - Add: CSRF token fetch on app load
      - Add: include X-CSRF-Token header on all mutations
    File: src/hooks/useAuth.ts
      - Update login() to not write JWT to localStorage
      - Update logout() to clear cookie (server-side via DELETE /auth/session)
    File: src/api/client.ts
      - Update all API calls to include X-CSRF-Token header

  BACKEND (Zoe Dev leads):
    File: src/middleware/auth.ts
      - Update: accept both JWT (legacy) and session cookies (new)
      - Add: session validation from Redis/in-memory store
      - Add: CSRF token validation for mutations
    File: src/routes/auth.ts
      - POST /auth/login:
          if AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED:
            create session, set cookie, return CSRF token
          else:
            return JWT as before
      - GET /auth/csrf-token: return fresh CSRF token
      - DELETE /auth/session: clear cookie on logout
    File: src/services/session-store.ts  [new file]
      - In-memory session store (upgrade to Redis later)
      - session_id -> { user_id, metadata, expires_at }

PROJ-201 -- Implement webhook retry logic with exponential backoff
Status: In Progress  |  Priority: High  |  Due: 2026-05-30
Assignee: @me (Zoe Dev)  |  Estimate: 8 pts  |  Team: Backend
URL: https://linear.app/zuno/issue/PROJ-201
Created: 2026-05-18 by Engineering Lead
Labels: backend, webhook, reliability, observability, sprint-2026-05

Description:
Webhook deliveries are currently fire-and-forget (no retry). If a webhook
target is down, the event is permanently lost. Customers have complained
about missed webhook events during short outages.

Implement retry logic with exponential backoff:
  - Retry schedule: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s (max 5 retries)
  - Max total retry window: 30 seconds
  - After max retries: mark as failed, log an alert
  - Persist delivery state to survive process restarts

Acceptance Criteria:
  [ ] Webhook events are retried on network failures and 5xx responses
  [ ] Webhook events are NOT retried on 4xx responses (permanent failure)
  [ ] State persists in database (webhook_events table)
  [ ] After max retries, event is marked failed and alert is logged
  [ ] Circuit breaker: target marked dead after 3 consecutive failures
  [ ] Idempotency: delivery_id in payload for deduplication
  [ ] All retry paths covered by integration tests
  [ ] Structured logs emitted for Datadog ingestion

Technical Scope:
  File: src/workers/webhook-retry.ts  [new file]
    - Main retry worker loop
    - calculateNextRetryAt(attempt: number): Date
    - isCircuitOpen(targetUrl: string): boolean
    - processWebhookEvent(event: WebhookEvent): Promise<void>
  File: src/types/webhook.ts
    - WebhookEvent interface
    - WebhookStatus enum: pending, success, failed, dead
    - DeliveryAttempt interface
  File: src/db/webhook-events.ts  [new file]
    - createWebhookEvent(event): Promise<WebhookEvent>
    - updateWebhookStatus(id, status, attempt): Promise<void>
    - getRetryableEvents(): Promise<WebhookEvent[]>
    - markTargetAsDead(targetUrl: string): Promise<void>
  File: migrations/0024_webhook_events.sql  [new file]
    - webhook_events table
    - webhook_targets table (for circuit breaker state)

Subtasks (3/5 complete):
  ✓ PROJ-201-1: Design retry queue schema  (2026-05-24)
      Output: migrations/0024_webhook_events.sql with all fields
  ✓ PROJ-201-2: Create WebhookEvent TypeScript model  (2026-05-24)
      Output: src/types/webhook.ts with full interface definitions
  ✓ PROJ-201-3: Implement exponential backoff timer  (2026-05-24)
      Output: calculateNextRetryAt() function tested with all 5 attempts
  ◯ PROJ-201-4: Write integration test suite  (planned: 2026-05-28)
      Scope: all retry paths, circuit breaker, process kill/restart
  ◯ PROJ-201-5: Add Datadog/observability integration  (planned: 2026-05-29)
      Scope: structured logs, metrics, trace IDs

Related:

  Related: PROJ-180 (observability stack setup -- Datadog)

Comments (4):

  @me (Zoe Dev)  2026-05-24 14:30
  --------------------------------------------------
  3 subtasks done! The exponential backoff handler is working in local tests.
  Verified backoff intervals:
    attempt=1  next_retry_at = now + 1s    (total window: 1s)
    attempt=2  next_retry_at = now + 2s    (total window: 3s)
    attempt=3  next_retry_at = now + 4s    (total window: 7s)
    attempt=4  next_retry_at = now + 8s    (total window: 15s)
    attempt=5  next_retry_at = now + 16s   (total window: 31s)
    -> cap at 30s: attempt=5 next_retry_at = now + 15s  (total window: 30s)
  Next step: write integration test suite.

  Engineering Lead  2026-05-25 09:11
  --------------------------------------------------
  Good progress! A few things to think about:

  1. Circuit breaker pattern:
     If a webhook target is permanently dead (3+ consecutive failures),
     stop retrying and mark the target as dead. Send an alert. Don't
     retry forever -- it wastes resources and hides the real issue
     (the target is misconfigured or the customer's server is down).
     Suggested: count consecutive failures per target_url. Reset counter
     on any success.

  2. Idempotency:
     If we retry the same event twice due to a network fluke, the target
     should be able to detect and reject the duplicate. Add a delivery_id
     (UUID) to each delivery attempt. Document in the API contract that
     targets should deduplicate on delivery_id.

  3. Process restart persistence test:
     Kill the process mid-retry, restart, verify events are retried
     with the correct next_retry_at. This is critical for reliability.
     Without this test, we can't be confident the DB state is correct.

  4. Consider: jitter on retry times to prevent thundering herd.
     If 1,000 webhooks all fail at once (e.g., during an outage), they'll
     all retry at the same time. Add ±10% random jitter to next_retry_at.

    Reply from @me (Zoe Dev)  2026-05-25 10:05
    ..............................................
    Great points! Adding all of these:

    1. Circuit breaker:
       - webhook_targets table tracks consecutive_failures per target_url
       - After 3 consecutive failures: update target.status = 'dead'
       - Alert: structured log + Datadog metric
       - Reset: on any successful delivery, consecutive_failures = 0
       - Manual re-enable via PATCH /admin/webhook-targets/:id

    2. Idempotency:
       - Add delivery_id = uuid() to each delivery attempt
       - Include in webhook payload: X-Zuno-Delivery-ID header
       - Document that targets should deduplicate on this header
       - Note: idempotency is the target's responsibility, we just provide the ID

    3. Persistence test:
       Integration test scenario:
         1. Create a webhook event
         2. Mock first delivery to fail (network timeout)
         3. Verify next_retry_at is set correctly
         4. Restart the process (kill + restart)
         5. Verify the event is retried at the correct time
         6. Verify attempt_count increments correctly

    4. Jitter:
       Good idea! Adding: next_retry_at += Math.random() * 0.1 * base_delay
       (±5% jitter so we don't overwhelm targets during thundering-herd
        recovery, but also don't delay too much)

  @alee (Aarav Lee -- Backend)  2026-05-25 11:30
  --------------------------------------------------
  One more thing to consider: what happens if the webhook_events table
  gets too large? After a long outage, we might have thousands of pending
  events. We should add a retention policy -- maybe purge events older
  than 7 days that are still in 'failed' state.

    Reply from @me (Zoe Dev)  2026-05-25 11:50
    ..............................................
    Good catch! Adding retention policy:
    - Failed events older than 7 days are purged
    - Scheduled job runs daily (cron: 0 0 * * *)
    - Purged events are archived to S3 (future -- for now just delete)
    I'll add this as a 6th subtask.

sessions.design_notes: EASY case — unambiguous overhead. Rich technical vocabulary (CSRF, httpOnly, circuit breaker, idempotency, React.lazy, exponential backoff, const enum, Suspense) that could trick a naive classifier. Discriminator: browser-only session (Linear + GitHub + Gmail), no code editor, no terminal, no file edits. Also covers the 'reading about tickets ≠ working on them' boundary for multiple tickets simultaneously (PROJ-210 and PROJ-201 both appear in detail but the session type is still overhead). A classifier that sees 'CSRF token generation' or 'circuit breaker' might incorrectly label this PROJ-210 or PROJ-201 task work.

  Zoe -- re: PROJ-210 pair session. One thing to consider for the CSRF token:
  if we store it in module-level state, React HMR in dev mode will reset it
  on every hot reload. That causes random 403s during development (you hit
  submit, it fires the mutation, CSRF token is stale because HMR just reset it).

  The ref persists across re-renders and only resets on a full page reload
  (which is correct -- full reload = new session = new CSRF token anyway).

Today 09:20  Zoe Dev (me)  [typing...]
  good point! useRef inside AuthContext provider makes sense.
  the flow would be:
    const csrfRef = useRef<string | null>(null)
    
    // fetch CSRF on mount
    useEffect(() => {
      fetchCsrfToken().then(({ csrfToken, maxAge }) => {
        csrfRef.current = csrfToken
        // schedule refresh at 90% of TTL
        const refreshMs = maxAge * 0.9 * 1000
        setTimeout(() => refreshCsrfToken(csrfRef), refreshMs)
      })
    }, [])
    
    // provide to children
    const getCsrfToken = () => csrfRef.current
  
  then all mutation calls do:
    headers: { 'X-CSRF-Token': getCsrfToken() }
  
  the only downside: getCsrfToken() can return null before mount

  ─────────────────────────────────────────
  import crypto from 'crypto'

  interface Session {
    sessionId: string
    userId: string
    email: string
    csrfToken: string
    expiresAt: Date
    createdAt: Date
  }

  const SESSION_TTL_MS = 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 // 30 days

  class InMemorySessionStore {
    private sessions = new Map<string, Session>()

    create(userId: string, email: string): Session {
      const session: Session = {
        sessionId:  crypto.randomUUID(),
        userId,
        email,
        csrfToken:  crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'),
        expiresAt:  new Date(Date.now() + SESSION_TTL_MS),
        createdAt:  new Date(),
      }
      this.sessions.set(session.sessionId, session)
      return session
    }

    get(sessionId: string): Session | null {
      const session = this.sessions.get(sessionId)
      if (!session) return null
      if (session.expiresAt < new Date()) {
        this.sessions.delete(sessionId)
        return null  // expired
      }
      return session
    }

    delete(sessionId: string): void {
      this.sessions.delete(sessionId)
    }

    validateCsrf(sessionId: string, csrfToken: string): boolean {
      const session = this.get(sessionId)
      if (!session) return false
      try {
        return crypto.timingSafeEqual(
          Buffer.from(session.csrfToken, 'utf8'),
          Buffer.from(csrfToken, 'utf8')
        )
      } catch {
        return false  // buffers differ in length → exception → false
      }
    }

    pruneExpired(): number {
      const before = this.sessions.size
      const now = new Date()
      for (const [id, session] of this.sessions) {
        if (session.expiresAt < now) this.sessions.delete(id)
      }
      return before - this.sessions.size  // pruned count
    }

    size(): number {
      return this.sessions.size
    }
  }

  export const sessionStore = new InMemorySessionStore()

  // zuno-app — auth middleware
  import { Request, Response, NextFunction } from 'express'
  import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken'
  import 'cookie-parser'  // augments express Request with .cookies
  import { config } from '../config'
  import { sessionStore } from '../services/session-store'
  import logger from '../utils/logger'

  /** HTTP methods that require CSRF validation on the cookie path */
  const MUTATION_METHODS = new Set(['POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'])

  export interface AuthenticatedRequest extends Request {
    userId?: string
    email?: string
    sessionId?: string
    authMethod?: 'jwt' | 'cookie'
  }

  /**
   * requireAuth: accepts JWT (legacy) OR session cookie (new, PROJ-210).
   * Cookie path is gated by config.AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED feature flag.
   * Cookie path validates CSRF token on mutations (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE).
   * JWT path is unchanged for backward compatibility.
   */
  export function requireAuth(
    req: AuthenticatedRequest,
    res: Response,
    next: NextFunction
  ): void {
    // --- Cookie path (new, AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED) ---
    if (config.AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED) {
      const sessionId = req.cookies?.['zuno_session'] as string | undefined
      if (sessionId) {
        const session = sessionStore.get(sessionId)
        if (!session) {
          res.status(401).json({ error: 'Session expired or invalid' })
          return
        }
        // CSRF validation for state-changing requests
        if (MUTATION_METHODS.has(req.method)) {
          const csrfToken = req.headers['x-csrf-token'] as string | undefined
          if (!csrfToken || !sessionStore.validateCsrf(sessionId, csrfToken)) {
            logger.warn({
              event: 'csrf_validation_failed',
              userId: session.userId,
              method: req.method,
              path: req.path,
            })
            res.status(403).json({ error: 'CSRF validation failed' })
            return
          }
        }
        req.userId    = session.userId
        req.email     = session.email
        req.sessionId = sessionId
        req.authMethod = 'cookie'
        logger.debug({ event: 'auth_ok', method: 'cookie', userId: session.userId })
        next()
        return
      }
    }
    // --- Legacy JWT path ---
    const authHeader = req.headers.authorization
    if (!authHeader?.startsWith('Bearer ')) {
      res.status(401).json({ error: 'Authentication required' })
      return
    }
    const token = authHeader.slice(7)
    try {
      const decoded = jwt.verify(token, config.JWT_SECRET) as {
        userId: string
        email:  string
      }
      req.userId    = decoded.userId
      req.email     = decoded.email
      req.authMethod = 'jwt'
      next()
    } catch {
      res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid or expired token' })
    }
  }

  3   interface Session {

  5     userId:    string

  7     csrfToken: string
  8     expiresAt: Date
  9     createdAt: Date
 10   }

 12   const SESSION_TTL_MS = 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000  // 30 days
 13   
 14   class InMemorySessionStore {
 15     private sessions = new Map<string, Session>()
 16   
 17     create(userId: string, email: string): Session {
 18       const session: Session = {
 19         sessionId:  crypto.randomUUID(),
 20         userId,
 21         email,
 22         csrfToken:  crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'),
 23         expiresAt:  new Date(Date.now() + SESSION_TTL_MS),
 24         createdAt:  new Date(),
 25       }
 26       this.sessions.set(session.sessionId, session)
 27       return session
 28     }
 29   
 30     get(sessionId: string): Session | null {
 31       const session = this.sessions.get(sessionId)
 32       if (!session) return null
 33       if (session.expiresAt < new Date()) {
 34         this.sessions.delete(sessionId)
 35         return null
 36       }
 37       return session
 38     }
 39   
 40     delete(sessionId: string): void {
 41       this.sessions.delete(sessionId)
 42     }
 43   
 44     validateCsrf(sessionId: string, csrfToken: string): boolean {
 45       const session = this.get(sessionId)
 46       if (!session) return false
 47       try {
 48         return crypto.timingSafeEqual(
 49           Buffer.from(session.csrfToken, 'utf8'),
 50           Buffer.from(csrfToken, 'utf8')
 51         )
 52       } catch {
 53         return false  // buffer length mismatch
 54       }
 55     }
 56   
 57     pruneExpired(): number {
 58       const before = this.sessions.size
 59       const now = new Date()
 60       for (const [id, session] of this.sessions) {
 61         if (session.expiresAt < now) this.sessions.delete(id)
 62       }
 63       return before - this.sessions.size
 64     }
 65   
 66     size(): number { return this.sessions.size }
 67   }
 68   
 69   export const sessionStore = new InMemorySessionStore()

  ─────────────────────────────────────────
  // zuno-app — auth routes
  import { Router, Request, Response } from 'express'
  import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken'
  import bcrypt from 'bcrypt'
  import { config } from '../config'
  import { db } from '../db'
  import { requireAuth, AuthenticatedRequest } from '../middleware/auth'
  import { sessionStore } from '../services/session-store'
  import logger from '../utils/logger'

  export const authRouter = Router()

  const COOKIE_NAME = 'zuno_session'
  const SESSION_TTL_SECS = 30 * 24 * 60 * 60  // 30 days

  /**
   * Cookie options for httpOnly session cookie.
   * Secure=true requires HTTPS -- set to false in development.
   */
  const cookieOptions = {
    httpOnly:  true,
    secure:    config.NODE_ENV === 'production',
    sameSite:  'strict' as const,
    maxAge:    SESSION_TTL_SECS * 1000,  // ms
    path:      '/',
    domain:    config.COOKIE_DOMAIN,
  }

  /**
   * POST /auth/login
   * Body: { email, password }
   * If AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED: sets httpOnly cookie + returns { csrfToken, expiresAt }
   * If not:                 returns { token } (JWT, legacy)
   */
  authRouter.post('/login', async (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const { email, password } = req.body as { email?: string; password?: string }
    if (!email || !password) {
      return res.status(400).json({ error: 'email and password required' })
    }
    const user = await db.users.findByEmail(email)
    if (!user || !await bcrypt.compare(password, user.passwordHash)) {
      return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid email or password' })
    }
    if (config.AUTH_COOKIE_ENABLED) {
      const session = sessionStore.create(user.id, user.email)
      res.cookie(COOKIE_NAME, session.sessionId, cookieOptions)
      logger.info({ event: 'login_cookie', userId: user.id })
      return res.json({
        csrfToken:  session.csrfToken,
        expiresAt:  session.expiresAt.toISOString(),
        maxAge:     SESSION_TTL_SECS,
      })
    }
    // Legacy JWT path
    const token = jwt.sign(
      { userId: user.id, email: user.email },
      config.JWT_SECRET,
      { expiresIn: '30d' }
    )
    logger.info({ event: 'login_jwt', userId: user.id })
    return res.json({ token })
  })

  /**
   * GET /auth/csrf-token
   * Returns a fresh CSRF token for the current session.
   * Only meaningful on the cookie path; JWT clients can ignore.
   */
  authRouter.get('/csrf-token', requireAuth, (req: AuthenticatedRequest, res: Response) => {
    if (!req.sessionId) {
      // JWT path — no CSRF needed
      return res.json({ csrfToken: null, maxAge: null })
    }
    const session = sessionStore.get(req.sessionId)
    if (!session) {
      return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Session not found' })
    }
    const remainingSecs = Math.floor(
      (session.expiresAt.getTime() - Date.now()) / 1000
    )
    return res.json({
      csrfToken:  session.csrfToken,
      expiresAt:  session.expiresAt.toISOString(),
      maxAge:     remainingSecs,
    })
  })

  /**
   * POST /auth/logout  (also DELETE /auth/session -- REST alias)
   * Deletes the server-side session and clears the cookie.
   * JWT path: no-op on server side (client deletes the token).
   */
  function handleLogout(req: AuthenticatedRequest, res: Response): Response {
    const sessionId = req.cookies?.[COOKIE_NAME] as string | undefined
    if (sessionId) {
      sessionStore.delete(sessionId)
      res.clearCookie(COOKIE_NAME, { path: '/', domain: config.COOKIE_DOMAIN })
      logger.info({ event: 'logout_cookie', userId: req.userId })
    } else {
      logger.info({ event: 'logout_jwt', userId: req.userId })
    }
    return res.json({ message: 'Logged out' })
  }

  authRouter.post('/logout', requireAuth, handleLogout)
  authRouter.delete('/session', requireAuth, handleLogout)

  /**
   * GET /auth/me
   * Returns current user info.
   */
  authRouter.get('/me', requireAuth, (req: AuthenticatedRequest, res: Response) => {
    return res.json({
      userId:     req.userId,
      email:      req.email,
      authMethod: req.authMethod,
    })
  })

  // --- PKCE state store (one-time use, 5-min TTL) ---

  interface PkceEntry {
    codeVerifier: string
    expiresAt:    Date
  }

  const PKCE_TTL_MS = 5 * 60 * 1000  // 5 minutes

  class PkceStateStore {
    private store = new Map<string, PkceEntry>()

    storePkceState(state: string, codeVerifier: string): void {
      this.store.set(state, {
        codeVerifier,
        expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + PKCE_TTL_MS),
      })
    }

    consumePkceState(state: string): string | null {
      const entry = this.store.get(state)
      this.store.delete(state)  // one-time use regardless
      if (!entry) return null
      if (entry.expiresAt < new Date()) return null  // expired
      return entry.codeVerifier
    }
  }

  export const pkceStore = new PkceStateStore()

  import crypto from 'crypto'
  import { pkceStore } from '../services/session-store'

  // Helper: base64url-encode a buffer
  function base64url(buf: Buffer): string {
    return buf.toString('base64')
      .replace(/\+/g, '-').replace(/\//g, '_').replace(/=/g, '')
  }

  /**
   * GET /auth/okta/redirect
   * Initiates the Okta PKCE flow: generates state+codeVerifier,
   * stores them, redirects to Okta authorization URL.
   */
  authRouter.get('/okta/redirect', (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const state         = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('hex')
    const codeVerifier  = base64url(crypto.randomBytes(32))
    const codeChallenge = base64url(
      crypto.createHash('sha256').update(codeVerifier).digest()
    )
    pkceStore.storePkceState(state, codeVerifier)

    const params = new URLSearchParams({
      response_type:          'code',
      client_id:              config.OKTA_CLIENT_ID,
      redirect_uri:           config.OKTA_REDIRECT_URI,
      scope:                  config.OKTA_SCOPES.join(' '),
      state,
      code_challenge:         codeChallenge,
      code_challenge_method:  'S256',
    })
    const authUrl = `https://${config.OKTA_DOMAIN}/oauth2/default/v1/authorize?${params}`
    logger.info({ event: 'okta_redirect_initiated', state })
    return res.redirect(authUrl)
  })

  /**
   * POST /auth/okta/callback
   * Receives the auth code from Okta, exchanges it for tokens,
   * creates a session and sets httpOnly cookie.
   */
  authRouter.post('/okta/callback', async (req: Request, res: Response) => {
    const { code, state } = req.body as { code?: string; state?: string }
    if (!code || !state) {
      return res.status(400).json({ error: 'code and state are required' })
    }
    const codeVerifier = pkceStore.consumePkceState(state)
    if (!codeVerifier) {
      logger.warn({ event: 'okta_callback_invalid_state', state })
      return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid or expired state parameter' })
    }
    // Exchange code + codeVerifier for tokens via Okta token endpoint
    const tokenRes = await fetch(
      `https://${config.OKTA_DOMAIN}/oauth2/default/v1/token`,
      {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' },
        body: new URLSearchParams({
          grant_type:    'authorization_code',
          code,
          code_verifier: codeVerifier,
          client_id:     config.OKTA_CLIENT_ID,
          client_secret: config.OKTA_CLIENT_SECRET,
          redirect_uri:  config.OKTA_REDIRECT_URI,
        }),
      }
    )
    if (!tokenRes.ok) {
      const body = await tokenRes.text()
      logger.error({ event: 'okta_token_exchange_failed', status: tokenRes.status, body })
      return res.status(502).json({ error: 'Okta token exchange failed' })
    }
    const tokens = await tokenRes.json() as {
      id_token: string; access_token: string
    }
    // Decode idToken claims (JWT, verify signature in prod via Okta JWKS)
    const idTokenPayload = JSON.parse(
      Buffer.from(tokens.id_token.split('.')[1], 'base64url').toString()
    ) as { sub: string; email: string; name: string }

    // Find or create user in our database
    let user = await db.users.findByEmail(idTokenPayload.email)
    if (!user) {
      user = await db.users.create({
        email:    idTokenPayload.email,
        name:     idTokenPayload.name,
        provider: 'okta',
        oktaSub:  idTokenPayload.sub,
      })
    }

    // Create session + set cookie
    const session = sessionStore.create(user.id, user.email)
    res.cookie(COOKIE_NAME, session.sessionId, cookieOptions)
    logger.info({ event: 'okta_login_success', userId: user.id, email: user.email })
    return res.json({
      csrfToken: session.csrfToken,
      expiresAt: session.expiresAt.toISOString(),
      maxAge:    SESSION_TTL_SECS,
    })
  })

Webhooks overview
Stripe uses webhooks to push real-time notifications to your application when
events happen in your account. For example, you can use webhooks to:

  - Fulfill orders when a checkout session completes
  - Notify customers when their invoices are ready

    Retry 3:   2 hours
    Retry 4:   5 hours
    Retry 5:  10 hours
    Retry 6:  24 hours
    Retry 7:  48 hours
    Retry 8:  72 hours (max)
  Note: Stripe's retry window (72 hours) is much longer than Zuno's (30 seconds).

  Because Stripe retries events, your webhook handler must be idempotent.

The digest() method of the SubtleCrypto interface generates a digest of the
given data. A digest is a short fixed-length value derived from some
variable-length input. Cryptographic digests should exhibit collision-resistance,
meaning that it's hard to come up with two different inputs that have the same
digest value.

  1   // zuno-app — webhook retry worker
  2   import { inMemoryWebhookStore } from '../db/webhook-events'
  3   import { WebhookStatus } from '../types/webhook'
  4   import logger from '../utils/logger'
  5   
  6   const MAX_RETRIES = 5
  7   const MAX_WINDOW_SECS = 30
  8   
  9   export function calculateNextRetryAt(attempt: number, now = new Date()): Date {
 10     const baseDelayMs = Math.pow(2, attempt - 1) * 1000
 11     const cappedMs = Math.min(baseDelayMs,
 12       (MAX_WINDOW_SECS - (Math.pow(2, attempt-1) - 1)) * 1000)
 13     const jitterMs = cappedMs * (0.95 + Math.random() * 0.1)  // ±5%
 14     return new Date(now.getTime() + jitterMs)
 15   }
 16   
 17   export async function processWebhookEvent(event: WebhookEvent): Promise<void> {
 18     if (event.attemptCount >= MAX_RETRIES) {
 19       inMemoryWebhookStore.update(event.id, { status: WebhookStatus.Failed })
 20       return
 21     }
 22     let statusCode: number | null = null
 23     let errorMsg: string | null = null
 24     try {
 25       const deliveryId = crypto.randomUUID()
 26       const res = await fetch(event.targetUrl, {
 27         method: 'POST',
 28         headers: {
 29           'Content-Type': 'application/json',
 30           'X-Zuno-Delivery-ID': deliveryId,
 31         },
 32         body: JSON.stringify(event.payload),
 33       })
 34       statusCode = res.status
 35       if (res.ok) {
 36         inMemoryWebhookStore.update(event.id, { status: WebhookStatus.Success })
 37         return
 38       }
 39       if (statusCode >= 400 && statusCode < 500) {
 40         inMemoryWebhookStore.update(event.id, { status: WebhookStatus.Failed })
 41         return  // 4xx: permanent failure, no retry
 42       }
 43     } catch (err) {
 44       errorMsg = (err as Error).message
 45     }
 46     // Schedule next retry
 47     const nextRetry = calculateNextRetryAt(event.attemptCount + 1)
 48     inMemoryWebhookStore.update(event.id, {
 49       attemptCount: event.attemptCount + 1,
 50       nextRetryAt: nextRetry,
 51       lastAttemptAt: new Date(),
 52     })
 53   }

Datadog Docs — Custom Metrics overview

Custom metrics
Custom metrics allow you to track application-specific KPIs that Datadog
doesn't collect automatically. Common use cases:
  - Business metrics: orders_placed, revenue_per_day
  - Queue depth: jobs_pending, retry_queue_length
  - Error rates: webhook_delivery_failed, circuit_breaker_triggered

Metric types:
  COUNT   — event count since last flush (e.g. webhook_delivery_attempt)
  GAUGE   — current value (e.g. webhook_retry_queue_depth)
  HISTOGRAM — distribution (e.g. webhook_delivery_latency_ms)
  SET     — count of unique values (e.g. unique_webhook_targets)

Submission methods (Node.js):
  Using DogStatsD / hot-shots:
    import StatsD from 'hot-shots'
    const dogstatsd = new StatsD({ host: 'localhost', port: 8125 })
    dogstatsd.increment('webhook.delivery.attempt', 1, { target: url })
    dogstatsd.histogram('webhook.delivery.latency_ms', latencyMs, { target: url })
    dogstatsd.gauge('webhook.retry_queue.depth', queueDepth)

  import { chromium } from 'playwright'

  async function measureFcp(url: string): Promise<number> {

    const page = await browser.newPage()
    const fcpPromise = page.evaluate(() =>
      new Promise<number>((resolve) => {

If a lazy-loaded component throws during loading, does ErrorBoundary catch it? Asked: 2022-11-15 | Views: 18,432 | Votes: 67 Top Answer (accepted, 189 votes): Mia Hoffmann Yes — ErrorBoundary catches errors thrown during React.lazy() loading. This includes network errors (failed to fetch the chunk) and errors thrown by the component itself on first render. Correct pattern: Error loading chart}> }> ErrorBoundary must be OUTSIDE Suspense -- if it's inside, Suspense won't suspend correctly because ErrorBoundary catches the Promise that Suspense needs to intercept. Important: ErrorBoundary must be a class component (React limitation as of 18.x). Hooks can't catch errors during render. The existing react-error-boundary package wraps this for you if you want a functional component interface. Second answer (72 votes): Luca Ferreira In React 19 there's a new 'use' API that simplifies this. But if you're on React 18, ErrorBoundary+Suspense is the standard pattern. (developer: confirms the ErrorBoundary-outside-Suspense pattern I already implemented in session 10. good -- nothing to change.) (quickly checks that the existing ErrorBoundary in the codebase is a class component) [15:17:55] Stack Overflow — TypeScript const enum tree shaking (developer switches to tab 2) URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/28818849/how-does-typescript-const-enum-work Question: What is the difference between 'enum' and 'const enum' in TypeScript? Does const enum actually improve tree-shaking? Top Answer (312 votes): Regular enum: compiled to a JavaScript object at runtime. enum Direction { Up = 'UP', Down = 'DOWN' } // compiles to: var Direction; (function(Direction) {...})(Direction = {}) // the Direction object exists at runtime, consumes bundle size const enum: inlined at compile time, no runtime object. const enum Direction { Up = 'UP', Down = 'DOWN' } // all uses of Direction.Up are replaced with 'UP' at compile time // no runtime object -- zero bundle cost For tree-shaking: const enum is better because bundlers (webpack/esbuild) see literal string values, not property accesses on an object. Caveat: const enum doesn't work across module boundaries with isolatedModules. (developer: confirms the PR #88 const enum fix was correct. Alex was right.) [15:18:35] Stack Overflow — timingSafeEqual length mismatch (developer switches to tab 3) URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51581920/node-crypto-timingsafeequal-throws Question: Why does crypto.timingSafeEqual throw when comparing strings of different length? I expected it to return false. Top Answer (89 votes): timingSafeEqual requires both buffers to have the SAME LENGTH. If they differ, it throws a TypeError. This is by design: - Constant-time comparison only makes sense for same-length inputs - If it returned false for different lengths, the length itself would be a timing oracle (attackers could probe until they find the right length) - So it throws to force callers to handle the case explicitly Correct pattern: try { return crypto.timingSafeEqual( Buffer.from(secret1), Buffer.from(secret2) ) } catch { return false // different lengths } (developer: yes, that's exactly what I implemented in session-store.ts validateCsrf(). the try/catch pattern is correct. just confirming.) > PROJ-230: the current exportToCsv() serializes all rows at once, which
  is slow for 50k+ rows (2.1s, 143MB). Add a chunked streaming version:
  exportToCsvStreaming(data, filename, chunkSize = 500):
    - Slice data into chunks of chunkSize rows
    - Serialize header from first chunk (includeHeader: true)
    - Serialize subsequent chunks without header (includeHeader: false)
    - Concatenate all chunks into one CSV string
    - Then create the Blob and trigger download as before
  This avoids serializing all 50k rows in one Papa.unparse() call.
  Keep the original exportToCsv() for small datasets.

● Claude is thinking...

  ✎ Editing src/utils/export.ts  (adding streaming variant)
  ─────────────────────────────────────────
  export function exportToCsvStreaming(
    data:      Record<string, unknown>[],
    filename:  string = generateFilename(),
    chunkSize: number = 500,
  ): void {
    if (data.length === 0) return
    const formatted = data.map(row =>
      Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(row).map(([k, v]) => [k, formatValue(v)]))
    )
    const chunks: string[] = []
    for (let i = 0; i < formatted.length; i += chunkSize) {
      const chunk = formatted.slice(i, i + chunkSize)
      chunks.push(Papa.unparse(chunk, { header: i === 0 }))  // header on first chunk only
    }
    const csv  = chunks.join('\n')
    const blob = new Blob([csv], { type: 'text/csv;charset=utf-8;' })
    const url  = URL.createObjectURL(blob)
    const link = document.createElement('a')
    link.href = url; link.download = filename; link.click()
    URL.revokeObjectURL(url)
  }

  1   // zuno-app — data export utilities

  4   function formatValue(value: unknown): string {
  5     if (typeof value === 'number') {
  6       return Number.isInteger(value) ? String(value) : value.toFixed(2)
  7     }
  8     if (value instanceof Date) return value.toISOString().slice(0, 10)
  9     if (value === null || value === undefined) return ''
 10     return String(value)
 11   }

 13   export function exportToCsv(
 14     data:     Record<string, unknown>[],
 15     filename: string = generateFilename(),
 16   ): void {
 17     if (data.length === 0) return
 18     const formatted = data.map(row =>
 19       Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(row).map(([k, v]) => [k, formatValue(v)]))
 20     )
 21     const csv = Papa.unparse(formatted)
 22     triggerDownload(csv, filename)
 23   }
 24   
 25   export function exportToCsvStreaming(
 26     data:      Record<string, unknown>[],
 27     filename:  string = generateFilename(),
 28     chunkSize: number = 500,
 29   ): void {
 30     if (data.length === 0) return
 31     const formatted = data.map(row =>
 32       Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(row).map(([k, v]) => [k, formatValue(v)]))
 33     )
 34     const chunks: string[] = []
 35     for (let i = 0; i < formatted.length; i += chunkSize) {
 36       const chunk = formatted.slice(i, i + chunkSize)
 37       chunks.push(Papa.unparse(chunk, { header: i === 0 }))
 38     }
 39     const csv = chunks.join('\n')
 40     triggerDownload(csv, filename)
 41   }
 42   
 43   function triggerDownload(csv: string, filename: string): void {
 44     const blob = new Blob([csv], { type: 'text/csv;charset=utf-8;' })
 45     const url  = URL.createObjectURL(blob)
 46     const link = document.createElement('a')
 47     link.href = url; link.download = filename; link.click()
 48     URL.revokeObjectURL(url)
 49   }
 50   
 51   function generateFilename(): string {
 52     return `zuno-report-${new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10)}.csv`
 53   }

  export function exportToCsvStreaming(

  ```ts
    const input = `${targetUrl}|${canonical}`
    return crypto.createHash('sha256').update(input).digest('hex')
  }
  ```

  key things:
  - sorted keys before stringify so field order doesn't matter
  - pipe-delimiter prevents ambiguity if url ends in json chars

   1   // auth-service-v2 — session middleware
   2   import type { Request, Response, NextFunction, RequestHandler } from 'express'

   4   export interface SessionStore {

   6     create(data: SessionData): Promise<string>

  10   export function createSessionMiddleware(
  11     opts: { store: SessionStore; cookieName: string; secure: boolean }
  12   ): RequestHandler {
  13     return async (req, res, next) => {
  14       const cookieHeader = req.headers.cookie ?? ''

  16         cookieHeader.split(';').map(c => c.trim().split('='))

  20       const session = await opts.store.get(sessionId)
  21       if (session) (req as any).session = session
  22       next()
  23     }
